quiz_questions.question_text: What is an Astronomical Unit (AU)?

options.text: The average distance from Earth to the Sun (~150 million km)
The distance from Earth to the Moon
The diameter of the Milky Way
The speed of light in one second

quiz_questions.explanation: One AU is the average Earth-Sun distance, approximately 150 million km (93 million miles). It is the standard unit for measuring distances within our solar system.

quiz_questions.question_text: A light-year measures:

options.text: The distance light travels in one year
The amount of time it takes for light to reach Earth
The brightness of a star
The age of the universe

quiz_questions.explanation: A light-year is a distance unit, not a time unit. It equals about 9.46 trillion km — the distance light travels in one year at about 300,000 km/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: Approximately how many stars does the Milky Way contain?

options.text: 200–400 billion
About 1 million
About 1,000
About 10 trillion

quiz_questions.explanation: The Milky Way galaxy contains an estimated 200–400 billion stars. Our Sun is just one ordinary star among hundreds of billions in our galaxy.

quiz_questions.question_text: The observable universe contains approximately how many galaxies?

options.text: About 2 trillion
About 100
About 1 million
About 200 billion

quiz_questions.explanation: Recent estimates suggest the observable universe contains about 2 trillion galaxies, each with billions of stars. The observable universe spans about 93 billion light-years across.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is the correct order from smallest to largest?

options.text: Solar system → Milky Way → Observable universe
Milky Way → Solar system → Observable universe
Observable universe → Milky Way → Solar system
Solar system → Observable universe → Milky Way

quiz_questions.explanation: Our solar system is a tiny part of the Milky Way galaxy, which is one of trillions of galaxies in the observable universe. The scale differences are enormous.

quiz_questions.question_text: The diameter of the Milky Way galaxy is approximately:

options.text: 100,000 light-years
100 light-years
1 million light-years
1 billion light-years

quiz_questions.explanation: The Milky Way is a barred spiral galaxy roughly 100,000 light-years across. Our Sun is located about 26,000 light-years from the galactic center.

quiz_questions.question_text: One parsec is approximately equal to:

options.text: 3.26 light-years
1 light-year
10 light-years
100 light-years

quiz_questions.explanation: A parsec (parallax-arcsecond) is the distance at which one AU subtends an angle of one arcsecond. It equals about 3.26 light-years or about 3.09 × 10¹³ km.

quiz_questions.question_text: The nearest star to our Sun is:

options.text: Proxima Centauri, about 4.2 light-years away
Sirius, about 100 light-years away
Polaris, about 1 light-year away
Betelgeuse, about 10 light-years away

quiz_questions.explanation: Proxima Centauri is the closest star to the Sun at about 4.24 light-years. It is part of the Alpha Centauri triple star system. Even at the speed of light, it would take over 4 years to reach it.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Sun is classified as a:

options.text: Medium-sized yellow dwarf star
Red giant star
White dwarf star
Blue supergiant star

quiz_questions.explanation: The Sun is a G-type main-sequence star (yellow dwarf) of average size and temperature. It has been shining for about 4.6 billion years and has roughly another 5 billion years of hydrogen fuel.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the Sun were the size of a basketball, Earth would be approximately the size of:

options.text: A small pea, about 26 meters away
A tennis ball, 1 meter away
A grain of sand, next to the basketball
A baseball, 100 meters away

quiz_questions.explanation: The Sun is about 109 times Earth's diameter. In a scaled model with a basketball-sized Sun, Earth would be a tiny pea about 26 meters (85 feet) away, illustrating the vast emptiness of space.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'Astronomical Unit' (AU)?

options.text: The average distance between Earth and the Sun, used to measure solar system distances
The amount of light a star emits in one year
The time it takes Earth to orbit the Sun once
The mass of the Sun in kilograms

quiz_questions.explanation: An AU (≈150 million km) provides a convenient way to express distances within the solar system. For example, Jupiter is about 5.2 AU from the Sun, and Neptune is about 30 AU.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'light-year'?

options.text: The distance that light travels in one year, approximately 9.46 × 10¹² km
The time it takes light to travel from the Sun to Earth
A unit of brightness for stars
The age of the Sun in years

quiz_questions.explanation: A light-year (ly) is a distance measure: light travels ~300,000 km/s, so in one year it covers about 9.46 trillion km. It is used for measuring distances between stars and galaxies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'parsec'?

options.text: A distance unit based on parallax, equal to about 3.26 light-years
A unit of time equal to one million years
A measurement of a star's temperature
The orbit of a planet around a star

quiz_questions.explanation: One parsec is the distance at which a star would show a parallax angle of one arcsecond when observed from opposite sides of Earth's orbit. Professional astronomers often prefer parsecs over light-years.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'observable universe'?

options.text: The region of space from which light has had time to reach us since the Big Bang
The part of the universe we can see with our naked eyes
The entire universe including everything beyond what we can detect
The solar system plus our closest neighboring stars

quiz_questions.explanation: The observable universe has a diameter of about 93 billion light-years. Beyond its edge, light hasn't had enough time (13.8 billion years) to reach us, so we cannot observe those regions — though the universe extends further.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'cosmic scale'?

options.text: The range of sizes and distances in the universe, from subatomic to billions of light-years
The weight of all objects in the solar system combined
A tool used to measure the temperature of stars
The gravitational pull of the Milky Way on other galaxies

quiz_questions.explanation: Cosmic scale refers to the immense range of sizes in the universe. From atoms (10⁻¹⁰ m) to the observable universe (10²⁶ m), the range spans about 36 orders of magnitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'scientific notation'?

options.text: A way of writing very large or small numbers using powers of 10
A type of handwriting used by scientists
A computer programming language for astronomers
The system of naming new stars

quiz_questions.explanation: Scientific notation expresses numbers as a coefficient times a power of 10 (e.g., 150,000,000 km = 1.5 × 10⁸ km). It is essential in astronomy where numbers range from incredibly tiny to unimaginably large.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'Oort Cloud'?

options.text: A hypothetical spherical shell of icy objects at the outermost edge of the solar system
A dense cloud of gas at the center of the Milky Way
A type of nebula where stars are born
A cloud formation on Jupiter

quiz_questions.explanation: The Oort Cloud is a theoretical region of icy bodies extending from about 2,000 AU to 100,000 AU from the Sun. It is thought to be the source of long-period comets.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'galactic' mean?

options.text: Relating to a galaxy or the Milky Way galaxy
Relating to Earth's oceans
Relating to the Sun's surface
Relating to the formation of planets

quiz_questions.explanation: Galactic means 'of or relating to a galaxy.' The galactic center of the Milky Way is about 26,000 light-years from Earth in the direction of the constellation Sagittarius.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'interstellar space'?

options.text: The space between stars within a galaxy
The space between galaxies
The surface of a star
The center of a black hole

quiz_questions.explanation: Interstellar space is the vast region between star systems. It is not completely empty — it contains extremely thin gas (mostly hydrogen) and dust known as the interstellar medium.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'parallax distance'?

options.text: A star's distance calculated from its apparent positional shift as Earth orbits the Sun
The distance a planet travels in one orbit
The width of a telescope's field of view
The distance between two stars in a binary system

quiz_questions.explanation: Parallax distance uses the formula d = 1/p, where p is the parallax angle in arcseconds and d is in parsecs. This is the most direct method for measuring stellar distances, reliable out to about 1,000 parsecs.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student says, 'A light-year must be a unit of time because it has the word year in it.' How would you correct this?

options.text: A light-year is a distance — it measures how far light travels in one year, about 9.46 trillion km
The student is correct — a light-year is indeed a unit of time
A light-year measures both time and distance equally
A light-year is a unit of brightness, not time or distance

quiz_questions.explanation: Despite having 'year' in its name, a light-year is purely a distance measurement. Light moves at ~300,000 km/s, so in one year it covers about 9.46 × 10¹² km. It is analogous to saying 'a day's drive' to describe distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer detects a galaxy 10 billion light-years away. What does this distance tell us about the light we are seeing?

options.text: The light left that galaxy 10 billion years ago, so we see it as it was in the past
The galaxy is currently 10 billion years old
The galaxy has been moving toward us for 10 billion years
The galaxy no longer exists because it was too far away

quiz_questions.explanation: Because light takes time to travel, looking at distant objects means looking back in time. Light from a galaxy 10 billion light-years away left 10 billion years ago. This is called 'lookback time' and makes telescopes time machines.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you could travel at the speed of light, it would take about 4.2 years to reach the nearest star. A student suggests this makes interstellar travel easy. What is the main challenge?

options.text: No spacecraft can currently reach anything close to the speed of light; current technology would take tens of thousands of years
The nearest star is too small to visit
There are no stars near our solar system
Traveling at the speed of light would destroy the spacecraft due to sound waves

quiz_questions.explanation: Voyager 1, our fastest interstellar probe, travels at about 17 km/s — roughly 0.006% the speed of light. At that speed, reaching Proxima Centauri would take about 73,000 years. Interstellar travel remains one of humanity's greatest engineering challenges.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher builds a scale model where the Sun is a golf ball (4.3 cm). In this model, where would the nearest star (Proxima Centauri) be placed?

options.text: About 1,100 km away — roughly the distance from New York to Chicago
About 1 meter away — across the classroom
About 10 km away — across town
About 1 cm away — next to the golf ball

quiz_questions.explanation: The Sun-to-Proxima-Centauri distance is about 270,000 AU. If the Sun shrinks to a golf ball (4.3 cm), the scale factor puts Proxima Centauri roughly 1,100 km away. This vividly demonstrates the enormous emptiness between stars.

quiz_questions.question_text: The James Webb Space Telescope can observe galaxies as they were 13.5 billion years ago. Why can't it see the very beginning of the universe (13.8 billion years ago)?

options.text: The universe was opaque for the first ~380,000 years — light could not travel freely until it cooled enough
JWST's mirror is not large enough
Those galaxies have already been destroyed
Light from that era has slowed down and stopped

quiz_questions.explanation: Before ~380,000 years after the Big Bang (the 'recombination epoch'), the universe was a hot, dense plasma that scattered photons. Once it cooled enough for atoms to form, light could finally travel freely — this is the oldest light we can ever see (the cosmic microwave background).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is confused about why the observable universe is 93 billion light-years across if the universe is only 13.8 billion years old. What explains this?

options.text: The universe has been expanding — space itself has stretched, carrying distant objects even further away
Light travels faster than we think
The universe is actually 93 billion years old
Scientists made a measurement error

quiz_questions.explanation: While light from the most distant objects left 13.8 billion years ago, the expansion of space has carried those objects much farther away during that time. The current distance to the edge of the observable universe is about 46.5 billion light-years in every direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mars is about 1.5 AU from the Sun. A radio signal from a Mars rover takes approximately how long to reach Earth when Mars is closest (about 0.5 AU away)?

options.text: About 4 minutes — radio signals travel at the speed of light
Instantly — radio signals have no travel time
About 4 hours — radio signals are much slower than light
About 4 days — space slows down signals

quiz_questions.explanation: Radio signals travel at the speed of light (~300,000 km/s). At Mars's closest approach (~0.5 AU = ~75 million km), the signal takes about 4 minutes. This delay means Mars rovers cannot be driven in real-time — they must be given instructions in advance.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Andromeda Galaxy is about 2.5 million light-years away and approaching our galaxy. If the two collide, when will this happen?

options.text: In about 4.5 billion years — cosmic events occur over extremely long timescales
Next year — galaxies move very fast
In about 100 years — it is already very close
Never — galaxies cannot collide

quiz_questions.explanation: The Milky Way and Andromeda galaxies are approaching each other at about 110 km/s and are expected to merge in roughly 4.5 billion years. Despite the 'collision,' individual stars are so far apart that direct stellar collisions would be extremely rare.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer measures a star's parallax angle as 0.1 arcseconds. Using d = 1/p, how far away is the star?

options.text: 10 parsecs, or about 32.6 light-years
0.1 parsecs, or about 0.326 light-years
100 parsecs, or about 326 light-years
1 parsec, or about 3.26 light-years

quiz_questions.explanation: Using d = 1/p: d = 1/0.1 = 10 parsecs. Since 1 parsec = 3.26 light-years, 10 parsecs = 32.6 light-years. Smaller parallax angles mean greater distances.

quiz_questions.question_text: A documentary says, 'There are more stars in the universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.' Is this claim scientifically supported?

options.text: Yes — estimates suggest about 10²² to 10²⁴ stars in the observable universe, which exceeds the estimated number of sand grains (~10¹⁹)
No — there are far more grains of sand than stars
No — we have no way to estimate either number
Yes — but only if you count asteroids as stars

quiz_questions.explanation: With roughly 2 trillion galaxies averaging 100 billion stars each, the observable universe contains approximately 10²² to 10²⁴ stars. Earth's beaches contain an estimated 7.5 × 10¹⁸ grains of sand. The star count wins by several orders of magnitude.
